9 min read
Congratulations! You’ve made it through the core teachings of
the Art of Email. You’re well on your way to creating something
incredible.
Now that you’re familiar with all the concepts, we’re going to
zoom out to the 30,000′ view and explain how to integrate the Art
of Email into your business in a way that’s best for you.
Before we do that, we need to reiterate that there is no one-size-
�ts-all ‘right’ way to implement AoE. Instead, there are two
options we recommend as starting points for everyone, and, from
there, an in�nitely rich tapestry of options to pursue based on
your speci�c needs and resources.
If most of your leads are not (paying) customers, the place to
begin is by creating a Relationship Building Series. Ideally,
everyone who enters your world without purchasing something
�rst will experience an RBS.
Few things in the Art of Email are non-negotiable. A Relationship
Building Series is one of those few things.
If you have multiple entry points into your world, prioritize the
Putting it All Together
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 14 --

largest source of leads �rst and start there.
(It’s likely you’ll be able to repurpose a lot of your RBS content for
everyone who enters your world with minor adjustments to
framing and other details if you have more than one RBS.)
If, on the other hand, the majority of leads are paying customers,
begin with a Customer Onboarding Series. A COS builds a
relationship with your audience within the context of getting the
most value from whatever was purchased. That’s a very powerful
frame (that creates an equally powerful opportunity to bond with
an audience).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 14 --

Once you’ve established your starting point, the next
consideration is how and when you intend to monetize attention.
Let’s consider a few examples.
Example #1 — you don’t have an offer yet and you’re building an
audience �rst.
In that case, we recommend creating a Value Newsletter
immediately after you’ve �nished your Relationship Building
Series. That’ll be your long-term platform to build and nurture
relationships, solicit feedback, and eventually announce a
promotion.
Example #2 — you have one evergreen offer.
In this situation, we recommend that you create a Story-Powered
Promotion and a Hard Bridge Email after your RBS. Then create
your Value Newsletter.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 14 --

With all of those ingredients — RBS, HB, SPP, and VNL — you’ll
combine critical elements of the AoE Trust and Revenue Engines.
But remember — the signal you get from sales in the �rst 90 days
is a fraction of the sales that will emerge over the long term (e.g.,
two years). DON’T optimize for those short-term sales —
recognize they’re a powerful signal that points to much more to
come.
Nurture relationships with your entire audience with Value
Newsletters and Soap Opera Series, and add another SPP (or two)
to create an SPP Stack over time.
Example #3 — you have more than one evergreen offer. (For
example, multiple digital courses, different service offers,
membership levels, etc.)
You’ll follow the same path described in Example #2 except you
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 14 --

may need a different RBS for each offer (if that’s how prospects
enter your world):
… or a single RBS for everyone with a “choose your own
adventure” Hard Bridge so your audience can self-select what
they’re most interested in.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 14 --

Another alternative, instead of leading directly into explicit
promotions, is to use an SOS ‘Showcase Series’ to introduce
prospects to your products in a way that more subtle.
Because it can take time to create multiple SPPs and other assets,
we recommend that you create your VNL immediately after your
RBS so you don’t miss opportunities to bond with and nurture
your audience when you’re building out revenue-generating
assets.
Example #4 — you have one (or more) time-bound offers.
The challenge in describing this scenario is that your priorities are
affected by when your time-bound offers are scheduled. If you
have 90 days before your next enrollment, for example, that’s very
different than if you have two weeks!
After you’ve created an RBS, create your Value Newsletter. If you
have time, create an SOS next that sets up your offer, then create a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 14 --

Hard Bridge, then write your PLS. (Don’t forget — a PLS is sent as
broadcasts so you can create that in real-time during the
promotion — that’s a great way to solicit and incorporate feedback
into your PLS emails.)
If you don’t have time to create an SOS you can skip that step and
send your audience 1-3 Hard Bridge emails leading up to your
promotion. (You can mention the promotion in your VNL too, but
you certainly don’t have to.)
If you have more than one time-bound offer, you’ll follow the
same format for each one (preferably including an SOS each time).
Example #5 — you have a time-bound PLS that you want to
transition to evergreen enrollment.
If you’ve already executed one or more promotions for a time-
bound offer, and you want to convert that to an evergreen offer,
follow the guidance in Example #2 and use the raw materials
from your PLS to create your SPP.
The ‘story’ in an SPP can be the story of your �rst enrollment, for
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 14 --

example. (That’s an easy way to get your feet wet writing an SPP.)
What did you learn, what have you changed/improved as a result,
how did Cohort 1 react to the product/service, what successes did
they create as a result, etc.
A successful PLS is fertile ground for creating an interesting SPP.
You’ll �nd lots of ideas for themes, hooks, and angles in the
feedback you received from that �rst audience.
Example # 6 — you have an evergreen front-end offer with one or
more evergreen back-end offers.
If you acquire customers with your front-end offer, create a
Customer Onboarding Series, VNL, an SOS that sets up your �rst
back end offer, and a Hard Bridge and SPP for your �rst back end
offer.
Repeat the SOS, HB, and SPP for each back end offer.
If you acquire leads before selling your front-end offer, after your
RBS create a Hard Bridge and SPP for your front-end offer (then a
VNL, and SOS + HB + SPPs for each back end offer).
Again, there is no right way to implement the Art of Email so
consider these examples as suggestions to kick start your thinking.
They’re not written in stone.
If your needs don’t �t neatly into the examples we’ve listed above,
be creative!
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 14 --

Here are a few guidelines for how to think about when
implementing AoE.
First — and most important — the majority of the content you
create should focus primarily on establishing and nurturing
relationships with your audience.
For perspective, between April 2020 (when our partnership began)
and October 2021 (when we released the Art of Email),
approximately 85% of the 200,000+ words of content we created
(not including our courses) was primarily for relationship-
building.
85%.
We realize that might seem overwhelming — and your needs may
vary. However, we recommend that, at a minimum, 60% of the
email content you create does not sell (explicitly).
75% would be better.
The simple, inescapable reality at the core of the Art of Email is
that most people who buy from you won’t buy in the short term.
That’s not our opinion, it’s a fact of life. Consider it the equivalent
of “gravity” in digital marketing. It doesn’t matter if we like
gravity or not — gravity just is, and we can choose to adapt our
business to it or not.
When we choose to make decisions based on reality, everything
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 14 --

changes. Worlds of new possibilities open to us. Every decision we
made in the Art of Email, and every decision we make in our own
business, is subordinate to accepting that we’re playing the long-
game because that’s how the world works.
That doesn’t mean we don’t try to make money in the short-term.
We know that some percentage of every audience will buy
quickly.
However, we also recognize those purchases are a signal of a
much larger opportunity that will emerge later, and that’s the
opportunity we’re optimizing for.
Second — in the Introduction to the Art of Email we mentioned
that AoE is based on systems theory. Now that you’ve learned
about all of the parts of AoE, we need to explain how to think
about those parts systemically.
In a linear view of the world, where A leads to B leads to C,
additions and improvements have the potential to create
incremental bene�ts.
If A is an ad, B is a landing page, and C is a three-part series of
promotional emails, improving any one of those parts is likely to
add a few percentage points to the whole (in the best case
scenario).
In a non-linear view of the world, however, it’s the relationships
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 14 --

among the parts that matter more than the parts on their own.
And each time we add something new to our AoE system, we
increase the number of relationships in the WHOLE system.
That means over time, as we add more parts to our AoE system,
we create opportunities for exponential improvement.
Like many aspects of systems theory, this is dif�cult to explain,
yet profound when it happens.
Rather than share a lot of complicated math, we’ll jump to the
punch line. If we were to ask you how much more complex a 16-
part system is vs. a 4-part system, you’d likely assume it’s four
times more complex because 16/4 = 4.
That’s how linear thinking works, and our brains are well-
equipped for that type of thinking.
However, systems theory shows us that a 16-part system is 20x
more complex than a 4-part system because of the comparative
number of relationships in the two systems.
Four parts interacting with three other parts (4 x 3 = 12
relationships).
Sixteen parts interacting with 15 other parts (16 x 15 = 240
relationships).
240 / 12 = 20.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 14 --

The math itself isn’t important (we like to say that �ve out of four
people are bad at math, and we’re de�nitely in that group).
But the math points the way to the larger point which is that every
layer you add to your AoE-inspired email marketing system
makes the whole system more powerful.
That’s true because the component parts of AoE’s Trust and
Revenue Engines were built purposefully to work together
systemically.
Once you understand and internalize this idea, you’ll begin to
realize the power that’s now in your hands.
Start by creating your �rst Relationship Building Series, then
choose whatever makes the most sense for your speci�c goals.
Then add another layer. And another…
Pay attention to your results because they will not improve in a
linear, incremental fashion. You’ll reach in�ection points along
the way when one new addition creates an outsize result. (That’s
what exponential improvement looks like and, when it happens,
that’s a powerful signal you’re on the right track.)
We’ll wrap up this module with an inspiring comment left in a
previous module. This is what the Art of Email is all about.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 14 --

Hey André and Shawn,
Thanks a lot of the new and improved Art of Email! I have a website I
have been working on and trying to turn into a membership. I knew I
needed a system in place and started an email series. Perfect timing
for the release of AoE. Reading through this has been tremendously
helpful. I have been pulling out weeds and redoing everything to �t
the lessons herein. I pulled out any selling talk and focused 100% on
providing value.
So far, I’ve only had a handful of folks go through it. Only 11 total, in
fact. Yet I already had someone enroll in the membership. As well, this
morning I received an email:
“Hi Jason
I took the 7 days for free and I am interested in the subscription, but I
have doubts about the duration of the routines, I would like to
incorporate mobility as part of my daily training, that is why I am
looking for a program of 10/15 minutes at most.”
This is valuable insight. I responded and told them NOT to enroll
(yet). Because I have actually been working on a similar program that
will be published in the coming weeks (that this email series sets the
stage for).
Like I said, I ripped out any sales talk to make it a true RBS. The last
day I mention that they will be added to the newsletter. And created a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 14 --

bridge by mentioning we do have a membership with exclusive
classes they can check out, if interested. But of course we have free
classes always available (here).
Not even any SPPs set up yet. My RBS has only been up for a week on
a tiny little website. ONLY 11 people have gone through this series so
far… And I already 1 membership enrollment and some great insights
for future programming. Wow. It’s amazing what happens when you
DON’T sell…
It’s a lot of work to set up, but I am already seeing results. I can’t wait
to see the long-term e�ects!
You guys ROCK. Thank you!
—Jason Jacques
Congrats for taking action Jason. We couldn’t have said it better
ourselves.
Please ask questions and leave comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
